data.title: From OpenClaw to RWA: How AI Proxy Agents Quietly Take Over On-Chain Assets
data.description:
In March 2026, Illia Polosukhin, co-founder of the NEAR protocol, made a seemingly simple yet profound statement in an interview: "The users of blockchain will be AI agents." He painted a vision of the future: AI will become the front-end interaction layer for all online transactions, while blockchain will retreat to the background, becoming a trusted back-end infrastructure. Humans will no longer need to directly operate wallets, browse block explorers, or check transaction hashes; these complexities will be completely abstracted away by AI agents. Almost simultaneously, the open-source AI agent project OpenClaw released version v2026.3.7-beta.1, implementing native support for GPT-5.4. This project, with over 280,000 stars on GitHub, released two major updates within two days. The official update log included a slightly self-deprecating yet confident statement: "We've fixed more problems than we created—that's progress." This update not only introduced a pluggable context engine but also strengthened security mechanisms and engineering deployment capabilities—OpenClaw is evolving from an experimental agent framework into a true "agent operating system." Meanwhile, another seemingly unrelated piece of news circulated in the crypto community: data from RWA.xyz showed that the on-chain value of tokenized real-world assets, excluding stablecoins, has surpassed $25 billion, nearly quadrupling from approximately $6.4 billion a year ago. The on-chain size of six major asset classes, including US Treasury bonds, commodities, and private credit, has all exceeded the $1 billion threshold. The fact that these events occurred so frequently within the same month is no coincidence. They all point to an emerging paradigm shift: when AI agents begin to interact autonomously with the blockchain, and when the scale of on-chain assets is sufficient to support an "agent economy," RWA's operating model will shift from "human management" to "AI autonomous management." This is an industrial leap that needs to be taken seriously.

I. AI is transforming from a "co-pilot" to a "driver"

To understand the depth of this leap, we need to first see the fundamental changes that the role of AI is undergoing.

Trade Execution: From "Instruction Response" to "Strategic Game Theory"

Current RWA trading mainly relies on manual order placement or simple smart contract condition triggers. Investors need to switch between multiple platforms, compare prices, assess liquidity, calculate costs, and then manually execute trades.

AI agents, on the other hand, can execute complex strategies. They can simultaneously monitor price differences across multiple on-chain markets and automatically execute cross-chain arbitrage; they can predict asset price trends based on macroeconomic data (such as interest rate decisions and inflation reports) and adjust their positions in advance; they can automatically execute stop-loss or hedging operations when preset risk control thresholds are triggered. More importantly, the game between multiple AI agents in the same market will give rise to complex dynamics that are difficult for humans to simulate—this is both a challenge and an opportunity to improve market efficiency.

Asset Management: From "Monthly Reconciliation" to "Continuous Monitoring" Managing RWA assets during their lifespan is often the most easily overlooked aspect. Daily operations such as rent collection, interest payments, collateral monitoring, and profit distribution rely on manual reconciliation and collection, which is inefficient and prone to errors. AI agents can achieve 24/7 uninterrupted monitoring. They can automatically allocate cash flow generated by assets to investor wallets; immediately issue margin calls when collateral value falls below a warning line, and even initiate liquidation procedures; and automatically handle early redemption, renewal upon maturity, and other operations according to preset rules in smart contracts. For investors, this means a significant improvement in the transparency and timeliness of asset management. Governance Participation: From "Low Voting Rates" to "Algorithmic Democracy" Tokenized assets typically come with governance rights, but traditional voting participation is extremely low. Most investors lack the time or willingness to delve into proposals, rendering governance a mere formality. AI proxies can change this. By analyzing proposal texts, assessing their impact on asset value, and simulating changes in returns under different voting outcomes, AI proxies can make decisions on behalf of investors. They can participate in governance continuously, rather than passively voting only at annual shareholder meetings. This makes governance a truly daily activity, not just an occasional formality. III. The Market is Already Voting with Real Money These may sound like predictions for the future, but market data is already confirming the trend. Data from RWA.xyz shows that as of March 2026, the on-chain value of tokenized real-world assets, excluding stablecoins, has exceeded $25 billion, nearly quadrupling from a year ago. The on-chain size of six major asset classes—US Treasury bonds, commodities, private credit, institutional alternative investment funds, corporate bonds, and non-US government debt—has all exceeded $1 billion. Traditional financial giants are accelerating their deployments. BlackRock launched the tokenized fund BUILD on Ethereum, Franklin Templeton migrated its US government money market fund FOBXX to the Solana public chain, and JPMorgan Chase processed billions of dollars worth of tokenized collateralized repurchase transactions through its Kinexys platform. These institutions will not easily enter a market without prospects. The competition between Circle and Stripe in AI agent infrastructure is particularly noteworthy. These two institutions, long positioned at opposite ends of the stablecoin value chain, are now penetrating each other's business areas. Circle is building application-layer infrastructure through the Arc L1 blockchain, the CCTP cross-chain transfer protocol, and the Circle Payments Network; Stripe, on the other hand, launched the x402 USDC payment function on Base for use by AI agents, acquired Bridge for $1.1 billion, and is jointly developing the Tempo L1 settlement chain with Paradigm. Artemis data shows that in January of this year, USDC on-chain transaction volume exceeded $8.4 trillion, and the entire stablecoin market has surpassed $300 billion. This is a financial scale sufficient to support the operation of an AI agent economy. Meanwhile, OpenAI's EVMbench, launched in collaboration with Paradigm, is testing the capabilities of AI agents in the field of smart contract security. Subsequent research showed that in EVMbench tests, the AI ​​agent was able to detect up to 65% of real-world vulnerabilities. Although the end-to-end exploitation success rate has not yet reached the level of human experts, this data is enough to attract the attention of the security industry. IV. Two Sides of the Coin: Great Opportunities, Many Pitfalls Any major technological change is accompanied by both opportunities and risks, and the integration of AI agents and RWA is no exception. On the opportunity side, efficiency improvement is the most direct value proposition. AI agents can operate 24/7 without interruption, unaffected by human physiological limitations; they can monitor hundreds of markets simultaneously, capturing fleeting arbitrage opportunities; and they can execute complex strategies that are difficult for humans to achieve. For asset management institutions, this means the possibility of reduced operating costs and expanded management scale. New business models are also emerging. "AI Agent as a Service" platforms may become the next growth driver: enterprises can rent professional AI agents to manage their RWA assets without building their own technical teams. New specialized agent service providers may emerge in niche areas such as cross-chain liquidity aggregation, automated market making, and algorithmic governance. Global liquidity is another promising dimension. AI agents can seamlessly access multi-chain markets, transferring assets between different blockchain networks, breaking down the liquidity barriers in the current RWA market caused by inter-chain fragmentation. When agents can freely move between different ecosystems such as Ethereum, Solana, and NEAR, the depth and breadth of the RWA market will significantly improve. However, challenges cannot be ignored. Security risks are paramount. AI agents hold private keys, execute transactions, and manage assets, meaning they become new targets for hackers. Vulnerabilities in private key management, algorithmic design flaws, and adversarial attacks can all lead to asset losses. EVMbench research shows that while AI agents perform well in vulnerability detection, their success rate in real-world end-to-end exploitation scenarios remains far below expectations. This means that current technology is insufficient to support fully unattended asset management. Compliance challenges are equally thorny. The legal status of AI agents is unclear: if an agent's erroneous decisions lead to asset losses, who should bear the responsibility? The developer? The deployer? Or the asset holder? Regulatory attitudes differ across jurisdictions, and the global accessibility of blockchain further complicates the issue. In mainland China, according to Document No. 42 jointly issued by eight departments, conducting RWA tokenization and related services within the country is illegal, and on-chain operations of AI agents must strictly adhere to this red line. Technological barriers are a real obstacle. For enterprises to embrace the AI ​​agent economy, they need both blockchain integration capabilities and AI deployment capabilities, which is a significant challenge for traditional enterprises. Cultivating a multi-skilled team, selecting suitable technology partners, and designing a robust governance framework all require time and resource investment.

V. Want to Get On Board? Do These Four Things First

Faced with the emerging AI agent economy, traditional enterprises and listed companies need to formulate a clear strategic path.

Step 1: Asset Digitization First

AI agents manage digital assets, not physical assets. Therefore, enterprises need to first tokenize their own physical assets (accounts receivable, equipment, property, intellectual property, etc.) through compliant channels. For mainland Chinese enterprises, this means paying attention to the filing channels in Hong Kong and other places, and exploring the RWA overseas path within the framework permitted by Document No. 42.

Step 2: Pilot AI Agent Nodes

No need for a full deployment all at once.

Step 2: Pilot AI Agent Nodes

No need for a full deployment all at once.

Enterprises can choose specific scenarios (such as cross-border payments, supply chain financing, and investor relations maintenance) as pilot projects, collaborating with mature AI agent protocols to introduce agents for automated management. Accumulate experience from small-scale pilots, evaluate results, and then gradually expand the application scope. Step 3: Cultivating a Multidisciplinary Team The AI ​​agent economy requires a cross-disciplinary talent pool. Enterprises need personnel who understand blockchain technology, engineers who know how to deploy and optimize AI models, and legal experts familiar with financial compliance. Cultivating or attracting such multidisciplinary talent is a guarantee of long-term competitiveness. Step 4: Participating in Standards Development The integration of AI agents and RWA is still in its early stages; technical standards, governance rules, and compliance frameworks are all in the process of formation. Forward-thinking enterprises should actively participate in industry discussions and promote the development of rules that benefit their own development. Conclusion: The Two Sides of Digital Civilization Are Quietly Converging. Looking back at the two events mentioned at the beginning of this article—OpenClaw's technological breakthrough and the leap in the scale of the RWA market—they seem independent, but in fact point to the same profound historical proposition. In the RWA Research Institute's cognitive framework, AI and blockchain have always been the two sides of digital civilization. One represents ultimate productivity, and the other represents advanced production relations. When AI agents begin to autonomously manage on-chain assets, these two sides are undergoing an unprecedented deep integration. AI agents process information, execute strategies, and participate in games with ultimate efficiency, while blockchain provides trusted asset registration, transparent rule enforcement, and trustless value transfer. This is not a simple technological superposition, but an evolution of economic organization. When assets are autonomously managed by AI agents, humans will retreat to the role of rule-makers and strategy designers. What social impact will this bring? How will governance power be distributed? How will the boundaries of responsibility be defined? There are no ready-made answers to these questions; they require joint exploration by industry, regulators, and academia. However, one thing is certain: the on-chain economy comprised of AI agents quietly began in a version update in March 2026. (This article is based on publicly available information, with data as of March 12, 2026. According to Document No. 42 jointly issued by eight Chinese departments, conducting RWA tokenization and related services within mainland China is illegal. The AI ​​agent on-chain economy discussed in this article only applies to overseas compliance frameworks and does not constitute any investment advice.)

data.title: Musk: Global GDP will increase tenfold in the next 10 years, barring a third world war.
data.description:
On March 11th local time, during a public dialogue at the "Abundance Summit" technology summit, Tesla and xAI founder Elon Musk discussed AI progress, the mass production schedule of the Optimus 3, and the post-singularity economic landscape. In this interview, he made several clear judgments: AI has entered a self-improvement phase, and humanoid robots are about to enter mass production. While the post-AI "singularity" economy is difficult to predict, he bets on deflation and that "money will no longer matter." When asked about the data center construction timeline related to SpaceX, Musk declined to elaborate, citing SpaceX's "quiet period." Regarding humanoid robots, Musk revealed that Tesla's "Optimus 3" is nearing completion: mass production may begin next year. He said, "We are completing the final stage of Optimus Prime 3, which is likely to be the most advanced robot in the world right now, and no other product has come close to its level." According to him, Tesla plans to: Start production this summer; initially, production will be low; and next year, high production is expected. Musk emphasized that robot production also follows the common S-curve growth pattern in manufacturing: slow initial growth followed by rapid expansion. He said, "Manufacturing output is typically an S-curve, starting slowly and then climbing rapidly." Meanwhile, Tesla is also designing new robot manufacturing plants. He revealed that the factory's design is significantly different from traditional factories, and the future goal is to continuously update robot versions, "possibly launching a new robot design every year." Regarding the pace of AI development, Musk's assessment is equally radical. When asked if AI has entered a stage of "recursive self-improvement," he stated, "Actually, this has been happening for some time now." He explained that current large-scale model development has formed a cycle: new models are trained using previous generation models, and humans are still supervising. But participation is decreasing. He said, "Humans are playing a smaller and smaller role in the loop. Each generation of models helps build the next generation of models." He predicts that this process may soon achieve a higher degree of automation: "Fully automated self-improvement may appear by the end of this year, at the latest by next year." In his view, AI breakthroughs have entered an accelerated phase: "The current situation is that I see an AI breakthrough before I go to bed at night, and another breakthrough when I wake up in the morning." After the AI ​​Singularity, "Money Will No Longer Matter" When discussing whether institutions can keep up with the impact of AI and robots, Musk used the "singularity" as a metaphor: "The singularity is called a singularity because it's very difficult to predict what will happen." He mentioned that the symbol of Grok is "the halo around a black hole," and said, "What happens inside the singularity is hard to know, but it will be very interesting." He directly attributed the inflation/deflation logic to a supply explosion: "We will have universal income (universal income)." Income), basically, is giving people money. The reason is that "the output of goods and services will far exceed the money supply," thus creating deflation: "Deflation is the ratio of output to money supply... If the growth rate of goods and services exceeds the money supply, you will experience deflation." Further afield, he believes the importance of money will decline: "Money will become less important at some point in the future." He even offers a pricing method for a "non-human economy": "I think future AI won't use human currency; it will only care about energy and quality—power and tonnage." As long as there is no third world war, global GDP will increase tenfold in the next 10 years. In terms of macroeconomic assessment, he provides a clearly defined optimistic range: he believes that there are "a series of possible outcomes, not all of which are good," but "it is very likely to be good," with a probability "possibly 80% or even higher." Without extreme exogenous shocks, he said his assessment of economic growth was "quite comfortable": "In the absence of a third world war... I think a tenfold increase in economic size within 10 years is a fairly prudent prediction." Employment and "Robots Building Robots": No layoffs, but rather increased recruitment, individual output will be "exaggeratedly high." Regarding the question of when robots will be able to participate in manufacturing on a large scale and replace humans, Musk emphasized that the reality is still that there are "a lot of people": Tesla has a total of "about 150,000 employees," of which "about two-thirds are in some form in the factory"; its supply chain-related workforce "may be between 1 million and 2 million." But his assessment is that efficiency will leap forward dramatically: "We don't plan to lay off or reduce staff. Instead, we will increase the number of employees." The real change will be in unit output: "Each person at Tesla will be nutty high." This echoes his description of the path of technological progress: whether it's AI or manufacturing, "it's often an S-curve, or a series of overlapping S-curves: slow at first, exponential growth, to a platform, and then another breakthrough that starts the next curve." Full interview translation: Host Peter H. Diamandis Viewers, as you can see, I'm still working hard to turn "hope" into reality. Musk, you look fantastic. Diamandis, I feel great. Musk, are you using some kind of anti-aging serum or something? Diamandis, this is our "Longevity Express," and we're moving towards that goal. You're on this path too. I think in our last conversation, you started to accept the idea of ​​extending lifespan. Musk: To some extent, yes. I don't know if we want everyone to live forever, but I think extending "healthy lifespan" instead of going through a long aging process and constantly drooling sounds like a good idea. We want to avoid that. Diamandis: First of all, congratulations on the collaboration between SpaceX and xAI. This is a fantastic move that will power the first-ever "Dyson Cloud." I'm curious about your timeline for launching these data centers? How much bandwidth do you expect to achieve in the first year? Please let us know how quickly you're getting this far. SpaceX is currently in a quiet period, and I can't reveal information that might cause trouble. Diamandis: Okay, let's not talk about that. I understand, but I'm excited about the pace. This Monday, we spoke here with Eric Schmidt and an executive from another hyperscale computing (cloud service) company. I won't name names, but I'm curious, where do you think we are right now in terms of AI's "recursive self-improvement"? Have we reached it? Do you think Grok is currently doing recursive self-improvement? How is it doing it? What is the timeline for Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) and Artificial Superintelligence (ASI)? Please give us a general overview. Musk: I think we've been in the recursive self-improvement phase for a while now. Do you mean fully automated recursive self-improvement without human intervention? Diamandis: Yes, I'm referring to AI software. Musk: Human involvement is indeed gradually decreasing during the recursive self-improvement process. Each new generation of models is built upon the previous generation. This has largely happened, but it's not yet fully automated. Perhaps by the end of this year, at the latest by next year. Diamandis Do you think there will be a smart "hard takeoff" (mutative development) by then? Musk We're in the midst of a hard takeoff right now. Diamandis Okay. Musk Right now. Diamandis Yes. Musk: At this stage, I go to sleep at night and AI makes a major breakthrough; when I wake up, there's another breakthrough. Diamandis: Indeed. Musk: To be honest, it's hard to keep up. It's definitely dizzying. Diamandis: I think many of those dizzying breakthroughs are also thanks to you.

MuskGrok is doing very well right now. It's the best in some metrics.For example, it's the best at predicting things, which is arguably the best metric for measuring intelligence. The new version of Grok is excellent.

We're currently lagging behind in programming capabilities. The reason I was late was because I just attended an all-hands meeting on programming, where we outlined all the work that needs to be done to catch up with and surpass our competitors in programming, and I believe we can do it. I think we'll probably achieve that goal by the middle of this year.

Furthermore, I believe that people do not fully understand how vast the intelligence of the future will be, or how it will surpass human intelligence to the point of being incomprehensible. You can imagine this scenario: Suppose we utilize a million times more energy than all the electricity currently consumed on Earth, that's still only about one millionth of the sun's energy output. Essentially, if you expand the US economy a million times, it still consumes only a fraction of the energy generated by solar power. If we truly calculate on a solar scale, even if you expand the current economy and electricity consumption by about a million times, you're still only utilizing about one millionth of solar energy. But what would an economy or intelligent entity using a million times more electricity than the entire current civilization look like? What would it think? What would it do? That would be a truly magnificent vision. The challenge we face is that even vaguely understanding that level of intelligence is difficult. But it's safe to say it could solve every problem you can imagine. Diamandis: Yes, it's like a long journey. You might find it a bit ridiculous, but I genuinely admire that relentless optimism. Musk: I see you're turning "hope" into reality. It's interesting that you've taken the phrase "turning hope into reality" to heart. Diamandis: That's Grok's doing. That was marketing advice Grok gave me when you were blasting me. Musk: Right? So you're monetizing hope. But you were also monetizing pain before. Diamandis: That's for sure. Musk: When AI and robots increase economic output by orders of magnitude, it's beyond our wildest imagination. Diamandis We are very likely to become one of the few intelligent beings on this planet in a very short time, then a very small minority, and finally an extremely small minority. Musk Yes. Not just on Earth, but throughout the entire solar system. Because if you only develop intelligence on Earth, the best outcome, that is, the energy that intelligence can utilize, is only about one billionth of the solar energy. If you are limited to Earth, that is your best outcome. Diamandis So that's the portion of energy we can intercept, right? Musk Yes. Earth receives only a small fraction of the sun's energy, which is the vast majority of the energy in the universe that we can access. Therefore, the intelligence of the entire solar system is actually many orders of magnitude greater than that on Earth. Diamandis Elon, may I ask you a question? How far into the future can you see? How many years into the future can you reasonably predict right now? Musk: It's very difficult to accurately predict the future path. Many things tend to follow an S-curve, or a series of S-curves. Development starts slowly, then grows exponentially, then enters a linear region, and finally becomes logarithmic growth (growth slows down). This is roughly what I've seen in AI breakthroughs. For example, after a breakthrough, it follows an S-curve, seemingly growing indefinitely, but then the gains decrease logarithmically until the next breakthrough. Therefore, the progress of AI is essentially a series of overlapping, interconnected S-curves. Diamandis: There was a time when you could predict what would happen ten or twenty years from now. What are your thoughts now? Musk: What I'm about to say might sound crazy. Diamandis: That's okay. Because you've always been an excellent audience for our bold predictions. Musk: Yes, I would say that in 10 years, the economy will be 10 times its current size or even larger. Diamandis: Yes. You did say that in five years or so from now, GDP will see triple-digit growth, and the economy will be 10 times its current size. But as far as your predictive abilities are concerned… Musk: I think a 10-fold increase in about 10 years is actually a rather conservative prediction. Unless something like World War III happens and disrupts these plans. But without World War III, if current trends continue, I would say the economy will grow tenfold in 10 years. Diamandis I like that statement. Can you give us an example? Musk Humans will establish a base on the moon. Diamandis Yes. And we will have humans… Musk land on Mars. Diamandis We will also build a mass projector on the moon. Musk I think so. I think we'll have a mass projector on the moon within 10 years. Diamandis That's awesome. Gerald K. O'Neil's space vision is coming true. At this year's Abundance Summit, we had four robots on stage together. I'm really looking forward to Optimus. I'm curious about the timeline for Optimus 3, especially when I can buy one or two? When do you expect Optimus to be commercially available? Or will you opt for a leasing model? Musk: We're currently in the final stages of completing Optimus Prime 3. It will be the most advanced robot in the world, unmatched by any other. In fact, I haven't seen any robot demonstration as impressive as Optimus Prime 3. Frankly, perhaps such robots exist, or they're kept secret, but I haven't seen them yet. Of course, I must make sure that what I'm saying is appropriately public. Diamandis: We're live-streaming this conversation on the X platform. Musk: Okay, that's pretty public now. Diamandis: Yes. Musk: I think we'll start producing Optimus Prime 3 this summer, but initial production will be very slow. Production numbers will follow a classic S-curve over time, reaching mass production sometime next year. After Optimus Prime 4, we'll accelerate design iterations. Next year, I might try releasing a new robot design every year and improving the design annually. Diamandis: When Dave Bundy and I toured the Gigafactory, it was an extraordinary experience. Tesla's factory is 11.5 million square feet. And then you said you were going to build a 9.5 million square foot factory there for Optimus Prime, which is amazing. Musk: The area figures are roughly like this. Diamandis: It has to be like this. Musk: That would be pretty good. It would be a completely new factory design, unlike any other factory. Diamandis: How far are we from "robots making robots"? You've already automated most of the processes at your Gigafactory, with humans playing only a small role. Will robots take over the current human role in manufacturing? Musk: We still have many human employees involved in manufacturing. Those directly employed by Tesla, those manufacturing products on the front lines, or those in the factory, either manufacturing products or managing product manufacturing. There are about 100,000 of them. So we have a lot of employees. Tesla's total workforce is around 150,000, and on average, two-thirds of them work in factories in some form. We probably have another one to two million people working with our suppliers. So the number of people involved is very large. We anticipate that Tesla's output per employee will become extremely high. Therefore, we have no plans for layoffs; in fact, we will increase our workforce. But human output per employee will become ridiculously high. Diamandis has reached unbelievable levels. When you came on the podcast, we discussed the concept of "sustainable abundance." You emphasized that we would be entering an era of "Universal High Income (UHI)," which goes beyond the scope of "Universal Basic Income (UBI)." I'd like to know what your thoughts are on how we can achieve this goal? Do you have any further thoughts? Furthermore, we previously discussed the possibility of a period of social upheaval, roughly two to five years. During this period, until we achieve “demonstration” and deflation, and thus reach the stage of universal high income (UHI), we may need to issue many relief checks similar to those during the pandemic. Have you thought more about this? People really need that kind of hope and vision. Musk: To be clear, I don’t think we should be complacent. We do need to be cautious because the future outcomes are varied, and not all outcomes will be good. But for now, I agree with you that the future is very likely to be good. There’s probably an 80% chance, or even more, that it will be good. I do think we will achieve universal income. Essentially, it’s about giving money directly to people. Because the output of goods and services will far exceed the money supply, there will actually be deflation. Deflation is the ratio of output of goods and services to the money supply. Therefore, if the growth rate of goods and services exceeds the growth rate of the money supply (which I predict will happen), then deflation occurs. Diamandis: Yes. Many people will start new companies and compete with each other, thus driving down prices, which will increase variability faster and exacerbate deflation. Musk: Basically, artificial intelligence and robots will produce so many products and provide so many services that humans will practically have nothing left to do manually. For humans, there will always be a moment when desires are satisfied. So, going back to my earlier example: if the economy were a million times larger than the US economy, then all human desires would have already been satisfied. Even if our economy were a thousand times larger than it is now, you might already have satisfied every imaginable material need. Diamandis So, do you think the value of money will decline significantly? Will we enter a post-capitalist era? Musk Yes, I think at some point in the future, money will no longer have any real meaning. Diamandis So just as you become… Musk The future of society might resemble something like the one described in Iain Banks' Civilization series. I think future artificial intelligence won't use human currency; it will only care about energy and quality—power and tonnage. Diamandis That sounds a bit ironic, doesn't it? Just as you're about to become a multi-trillion-dollar billionaire, money starts to lose its value. Musk: Pretty much. All these wealth figures actually only represent my equity stake in the companies I've founded. This money isn't in a bank account. Strictly speaking, I only own a portion of these companies. These companies are doing a lot of useful things, and as they create value, their valuations grow, and the equity stake translates into my total wealth, making the number look high. Diamandis: Someone once interviewed me and asked me what my motivation was. What drives you? I answered that Elon's motivation is problem-solving. He's dedicated to making the world a better place by solving the biggest problems time and time again. If someone else could solve these problems, he wouldn't have had to do it himself, but the problem is, no one else is solving them. So, I just want to thank you. Musk: You're welcome. Diamandis: I'm curious, do you think democracy and modern institutions can keep up with this impending "supersonic tsunami"? Will they be swept away by the tide of time? Will they collapse completely? How should we deal with it? Musk: There's a reason this is called a "singularity." It's difficult to predict what will happen inside a singularity. Grok's symbol is the singularity. Diamandis I really like it. By the way, the logo behind you is beautiful, very gorgeous. Musk Thanks. That halo symbolizes the mass and light around the black hole falling into it. It's hard to know what will happen inside the singularity, but it will be very interesting. I'm confident that the future will be very exciting. Frankly, I think artificial intelligence and robotics are also the only way we can solve the budget deficit and avoid national bankruptcy. You've had some influence on my views, and I've decided to become more optimistic now. We really should be more optimistic. Diamandis Thanks, man. Musk: I wasn't an optimist before; maybe I was too caught up in the negativity. Diamandis: Combining optimism and realism is always beneficial. Musk: Absolutely right. You can't be complacent, nor can you simply assume everything will go smoothly; you have to strive to make things work for the better. I mean, something amazing is bound to happen in the future. If we have highly dexterous and incredibly intelligent bionic robots, it means everyone on Earth will have access to better healthcare than the richest people in the world today. By the way, everyone says I'm the richest person, but I think those monarchs are actually much richer than me. For example, I've had to have three neck surgeries because the first two were botched. And my back still hurts a bit. I was thinking, could artificial intelligence help solve back pain? If so, that would be a huge win. And I think it definitely can. Back pain is really awful. It leads to poor sleep quality and makes people irritable. This morning, David Sinclair came to our stage. He's conducting clinical human trials of epigenetic reprogramming. A recently published paper shows that this therapy can achieve joint repair. Therefore, back pain might be one of the ailments it can eliminate. Musk: That would be truly remarkable. Of course, if it were just about solving back pain, the average human happiness would be greatly improved. Because with back pain, it's not a question of whether you'll get it, but rather sooner or later. There are indeed some flaws in the design of the human body. Diamandis: I've been wanting to invite you to Fountain Life Medical Center in Dallas. We'll help you. Please come whenever you have time. Musk: What kind of equipment do you have there? I understand you have MRI and CT scans, but what do you do with the scan results? Diamandis: I'd be happy to send you the service list; I'll DM you. You're so generous. Next up with me is another great "moonshot" entrepreneur, Ben Lamm. He runs Colossal, a company that "revives extinct species," and is researching the resurrection of mammoths and 15 other species. I heard you want a miniature mammoth, is that true? Musk: Yes, I think having a miniature mammoth as a pet would be incredibly cool. That would be absolutely epic. Just imagining those furry, adorable little guys running around and neighing is exhilarating. Diamandis Okay, I'll put in a good word for Ben. That's amazing. Musk Can anyone build a real-life Jurassic Park? If it were built, even with the risk of death, I would definitely go see it; it would be so cool. Diamandis I think if anyone could do that, it would be Ben Ram and his Colossal company. He's creating life through engineering. Recently, someone asked him if he could build a Pikachu, and he said maybe. Musk: Yes, Jurassic World or something, that would be amazing. Diamandis: Okay, I'll ask him. Elon, thank you so much for coming here to share with us. Thank you, my friend. Let's give Elon Musk a round of applause! (Background voice: Nothing can stop us)

data.title: The End of the App Era: A Major Reshuffling of Software Interaction Brought About by AI Agents
data.description:
In the past mobile internet era, our digital lives were fragmented across countless icons (apps). To complete a simple cross-platform asset transaction, we had to repeatedly switch between different apps, enduring varying UI logic and cumbersome operation processes.

But with AI agents, represented by OpenClaw (Lobster), stepping into the limelight, this "app-centric" logic is rapidly coming to an end.

However, with AI agents like OpenClaw (Lobster) taking center stage, this "app-centric" logic is rapidly coming to an end.

I. Intent Layer: Semantics as Operation, UI Becomes a "Backup" In the past, we manipulated "paths"; now, we express "intents." In the traditional GUI (Graphical User Interface) era, completing a task required an extremely high "learning cost." You needed to know where the buttons were and what level of menus they were in. But in the Agent era, interaction has become LUI (Language User Interface). Case Comparison: From "Click" to "Intent" Uniswap (DEX): Represents permissionless decentralized trading, but its complex wallet connections, chain switching, and slippage settings often cause headaches. OKX (CEX): Represents efficient centralized services, but the asset transfer process between CEXs and on-chain DEXs is extremely fragmented. The "torture" of traditional UI/UX: 1. Open the OKX APP, click "Withdraw," enter the address, and wait for confirmation. 2. Open the wallet plugin and connect to the Uniswap website. 3. Manually select a network and search for the token contract. 4. Set slippage, click Swap, and confirm the signature in your wallet. Semantic Interaction of the AI ​​Agent: Human: "Please exchange 1 ETH in OKX for the cheapest USDC on Uniswap, the lower the fee, the better." Agent: "Okay. I've detected that withdrawals from OKX to the X Layer have the lowest fees, and Uniswap currently offers the best liquidity at that layer."

Performing cross-chain withdrawal and automatic exchange for you...

Exchange complete. 1 ETH has been exchanged for 2026 USDC, saving you approximately $12 in gas fees. ”

This is the divine subversion of "semantic intent transformation". When conversation is a service, the carefully designed UI of an app will lose its meaning, because the Agent is the one who reads the interface for you.

II. Workflow Layer: Skill Encapsulation and Automatic Evolution If the intent is the "starting point" and the operation is the "end point," then the core value of an Agent lies in how to automatically build the "bridge" in between. Delving into the logic layer, an Agent is not merely a chatbot; it is an executor possessing a library of Skills. For the above case, the Agent automatically breaks down the following algorithm flow: Asset Awareness: Checks OKX account balance via API calls. Path Search: Compares OKX withdrawal fees with Uniswap's quotes on different chains. Dynamic Execution: Automatically handles Skill operations such as withdrawal verification and on-chain signing. Feedback Loop: Real-time monitoring of on-chain status and reporting results in natural language. Core Concept: Previously, these steps required engineers to "hardcode" the logic, but future Agents can automatically adjust Skill priorities based on environmental changes (such as sudden congestion on a chain), achieving logical self-evolution.

III. Architecture Layer: MCP Protocol and the Arrival of an "Agent-Friendly" Society

When software is no longer designed for humans, the underlying protocols of the Internet will undergo a major reshuffle.

data.title: With OpenClaw experiencing frequent security risks, how can shrimp farming be carried out with peace of mind?
data.description: Recently, OpenClaw, nicknamed "Lobster," has rapidly gained popularity due to its powerful features, becoming a new tool for many to improve their daily work efficiency. However, behind this trend, various security risks have also emerged, ranging from data breaches to attackers remotely taking over devices and causing irreversible losses. According to public research by the security firm STRIKE, over 40,000 OpenClaw instances have been exposed to the public internet, with 63% containing exploitable vulnerabilities, and over 12,000 instances marked as remotely controllable. Once OpenClaw is exposed to the internet and scanned by attackers, this "naked lobster" will be completely defenseless, and users' private messages, account credentials, API keys, and other sensitive information could be easily obtained, causing serious losses. Yesterday, we shared an installation guide on how to install OpenClaw. Today we'll thoroughly analyze OpenClaw's typical security risk cases, core risk classifications, and the most detailed security deployment steps to help you avoid pitfalls and use this tool safely and correctly. I. OpenClaw Core Security Risks We've categorized OpenClaw's security risks into 5 main types to help you quickly identify risk points: OpenClaw may grant excessively high privileges by default. Combined with the fact that many people are accustomed to running it with administrator privileges, once attacked, attackers can directly gain the highest level of control over the system, arbitrarily tamper with files, steal data, and even control the entire device. Network Exposure Risks Many people, after installing OpenClaw, fail to configure network isolation, exposing its management port directly to the public internet. Combined with weak passwords and disabled authentication, this effectively opens the door for attackers, making remote control and mass intrusion easy. Skills/Supply Chain Risks OpenClaw's "skills" extensions are very useful, but downloading unapproved skills or cracked plugins from unofficial channels can easily implant malicious code, leading to privilege escalation and data theft—essentially installing "monitoring" on your device. Risk of Uncontrolled Model Behavior Due to misunderstandings of instructions or being misled by malicious prompts, "misoperations" may occur—such as accidentally deleting important emails, clearing cache, or even performing unauthorized operations, causing irreversible damage. Configuration and Update Risks Many people neglect version updates after installation, allowing attackers to exploit high-risk vulnerabilities in older versions. Furthermore, without log auditing and basic hardening, it's impossible to trace the source of security issues and respond quickly. II. How to Securely Deploy OpenClaw (Lobster) In fact, by focusing on the four core principles of "isolation, least privilege, official source, and strong authentication," security risks can be significantly reduced. Below are six practical deployment steps that can be applied directly by both beginners and experienced users: Step 1: Isolate the Environment, Guarding the First Line of Defense Do not run OpenClaw on your primary machine (the computer used for daily office work and storing sensitive data). Prioritize using an idle, isolated computer, or run it using VMware, Hyper-V virtual machines, or Docker containers to completely isolate sensitive data from your primary system. Simultaneously, configure network isolation: bind the OpenClaw management port only to 127.0.0.1 (local access), prohibiting direct exposure to the public network; if remote access is required, it must be through a VPN or SSH tunnel, plus an IP whitelist, allowing only specified devices to access it. Additionally, remember to update your computer system and kernel, enable the firewall, disable unnecessary ports and services, and perform basic hardening. Step Two: Obtain from official channels, reject unofficial sources. This is the most crucial step! You must download the installation package from the official OpenClaw website (openclaw.ai) or the official GitHub, rejecting all unofficial channels such as third-party websites, cloud storage sharing, and paid installation services. After downloading, be sure to verify the signature or hash value of the installation package to ensure it has not been tampered with. After installation, immediately execute the "openclaw update" command to upgrade to the latest stable version (recommended version ≥2026.2.26, which has fixed high-risk vulnerabilities such as ClawJacked), and run "openclaw security audit --deep" for a security self-check. Step 3: Configure permissions and authentication to safeguard your access rights. Strictly prohibit running OpenClaw with administrator privileges. Only grant it the necessary file access and API permissions to complete its tasks, and restrict its access to sensitive directories (such as desktop, documents, bank statements, etc.) and high-risk system commands. **Set up strong authentication:** Passwords must be at least 16 characters long, containing uppercase and lowercase letters, numbers, and special symbols. Avoid weak passwords. Enable two-factor authentication (2FA), enable the access whitelist, and disable the default weak authentication method. **Additionally, for high-risk operations such as deleting, modifying, or sending sensitive data, mandatory secondary manual confirmation must be set up to prevent accidental or malicious operations. **Step 4: Standardize plugin/skill management and reject "toxic" extensions** Only download approved skills from the official OpenClaw skill platform, ClawHub. Never install third-party, cracked, or paid customized skills—these skills are highly likely to contain malicious code. Even for official skills, it's recommended to perform code scanning and permission checks before installation. For non-core, infrequently used skills, enable sandbox mode to prevent them from acquiring excessive privileges. Step 5: Implement runtime protection and monitor for anomalies in real time. If running OpenClaw with Docker, always run it as a non-root user, set a read-only file system, remove dangerous permissions, and restrict the container's resource usage and network access to prevent the entire system from being compromised. Enable full operation logging and regularly review audit records. If any abnormal operations are detected (such as batch file deletion, external key distribution, or frequent access to sensitive directories), immediately trigger an alert and block the operation. If you need to use OpenClaw to process sensitive data, first perform data anonymization (e.g., hide ID card numbers, mobile phone numbers), enable encryption during data transmission and storage, and prohibit storing keys and environment variables in plaintext. Step Six: Prepare for emergencies and maintenance. Regularly back up OpenClaw configuration files and important data, and develop isolation and recovery plans. In the event of a security issue, you can quickly recover data and isolate affected devices. Pay close attention to official OpenClaw announcements and CVE vulnerability information, install security patches promptly, and avoid prolonged use of older versions. If you notice any device anomalies (such as slow performance or the appearance of unfamiliar processes), immediately disconnect from the network, terminate the OpenClaw process, reset all access credentials, trace the source of the attack, and harden your security. In summary, OpenClaw security is never a matter of "installation is complete," but rather a process that permeates the entire workflow of "installation-configuration-use-maintenance." Remember these five key words: isolation, least privilege, official source, strong authentication, and strong auditing. It is recommended that you test and verify in an isolated environment first to confirm there are no security issues before gradually deploying it. After all, tools are meant to improve efficiency, not to cause data breaches or device hijacking due to momentary carelessness.

data.title: OpenClaw's shrimp farming craze is expanding: Offline creator workshops are about to open.
data.description:
In recent weeks, OpenClaw, once confined to the geek community, has rapidly expanded beyond its core audience. Within the developer community, it's affectionately known as "Lobster," and "lobster farming"—training and debugging AI agents to perform complex tasks—has become one of the hottest practices in the AI ​​era.

This trend is extending from the developer community to financial research and policy perspectives. Financial institutions have adopted it as a powerful tool for investment research, while universities and regulatory agencies are frequently warning of risks related to access control and data security. The explosion of OpenClaw is a microcosm of AI agents moving from "auxiliary tools" to "core productivity tools," and it also makes a core question increasingly clear: How will AI reshape the way we work in the future?

From Tool to Productivity

AI is completing a crucial leap: from "responding to instructions" to "actively executing."

AI agents, exemplified by OpenClaw, can not only understand instructions but also autonomously allocate resources and connect processes to achieve task closure. They enable individuals to build automated workflows, fostering entirely new production methods. However, amidst this hype, key challenges for implementation are becoming apparent: How to train truly usable agents? How to design secure and stable workflows? How to balance efficiency and risk? These questions cannot be fully answered through online discussions; offline experience sharing and practical exchanges become crucial.

An offline creator workshop for "shrimp players"

Against this backdrop, the "OpenClaw Creator Workshop • Skills Bazaar," jointly launched by Jinse Finance × Twinkle × Shuan Academy, will officially open on March 17th from 13:30 to 17:30.

This is not a traditional technical lecture, but a "skills market" focusing on practical application.

The event will focus on four core topics, directly addressing the pain points of AI Agent implementation: How can AI Agents be integrated into real-world workflows to double efficiency? OpenClaw's practical experience and techniques in development and automation. How can AI tools empower creators to overcome productivity bottlenecks? How to build a strong security defense and mitigate data and permission risks when using AI Agents? For experienced AI users, this is an offline platform to exchange advanced techniques and resolve difficulties; for beginners, it's a shortcut to quickly get started and understand AI Agent application scenarios. In the AI ​​era, everyone needs their own "lobster." This workshop is reportedly just the first stop in a series of events. The organizers plan to continue the series in eight core cities—Beijing, Shanghai, Shenzhen, Hangzhou, Chengdu, Hong Kong, Singapore, and Dubai—over the next few months, bringing the best practices of the global AI Agent community to more developers. Technological development has never stopped at tools; it relies even more on sharing and co-creation. The arrival of the AI ​​Agent era is no exception. OpenClaw's "lobster-raising craze" is essentially a preview of future work methods. AI is no longer just a chat window, but a digital assistant capable of executing tasks and managing processes. The process of "raising lobsters" is precisely the process of learning to coexist with AI and mastering future productivity. The AI ​​Agent era has arrived. The OpenClaw Creator Workshop on March 17th marks a crucial juncture in this transformation. It may simply be a technical exchange, but for everyone eager to embrace AI opportunities, it could be a key step in understanding AI and taking control of the future. This offline gathering for "Lobster Players" awaits your participation.

data.title: Bank card payments vs. stablecoin payments: Which is better for smart agents?
data.description:
Author: Jack Simison, NascentTranslated by: Yangz, Techub News

Last Friday, I had a barbecue with one of the maintainers of OpenClaw to discuss which payment channels smart agents should use. The debate lasted only about 30 seconds. He said:

"Anything will do as long as it's not cryptocurrency."

This isn't without reason.

After being plagued by discussions surrounding Moltbook and a series of token issuances, OpenClaw's Discord server has completely banned mentions of cryptocurrency. Every serious discussion about intelligent agent architecture ultimately ends up being accompanied by a token's price code. Therefore, banning the topic is a reasonable response. This association gives serious non-cryptocurrency developers ample reason to view "cryptocurrency" as a burden, not infrastructure. If you're launching an AI product in 2026, the last thing you want is for your payment layer to be burdened by speculation. But this reaction masks a crucial difference. Stablecoins are not speculative tokens. They have no issuance ceremony. They don't go to zero. They are a unit of value that can be settled without banks, transferred without wire transfers, and costs less than a penny per transaction. Peter Steinberger's decision to integrate OpenClaw with CreditClaw (a virtual card solution powered by Stripe) immediately drew criticism from the cryptocurrency community. In 2026, many considered relying on traditional card payment infrastructure obsolete, accusing him of a flawed approach. However, for its specific use case, he was correct. Steinberger's choice to use bank cards was correct. When intelligent agents act as human proxies to do things humans want to do but don't want to do themselves, bank card payment channels are indeed the right choice. The design intent of bank card networks is very clear: to make it convenient for people to use their funds. Users open accounts with issuing banks, which provide cards and credit limits (for credit cards) or linked debit balances (for debit cards). The bank card network then routes transaction information between the issuing bank and the merchant's acquiring bank. The network and issuing institutions are responsible for enforcing transaction policies and rules and handling final settlement. Most notably, they employ network tokenization technology, replacing the original card number with a merchant-specific token. These tokens act as secure substitutes for the underlying card and prevent the card's token from being used by other merchants (which is highly beneficial for agent control). These networks are deeply optimized for consumer protection. Refund, fraud revocation, and dispute resolution mechanisms have been refined over decades and operate on a large scale globally. If an agent books a flight and the route changes, the consumer has recourse. If a stolen bank card is used for shopping, the issuing bank can reverse the fraudulent transaction. If a consumer buys a laptop online but never receives it, they can dispute the charge and recover the funds. This layer of security is the cornerstone of how bank card payment systems operate. Another advantage is merchant acceptance. Visa and Mastercard are accepted at over 150 million locations worldwide. Stablecoin acceptance is only a tiny fraction of that, primarily limited to cryptocurrency-native merchants and specific payment processors. For any agent performing tasks within the existing economic system—such as booking travel, ordering supplies, paying SaaS bills, or purchasing software licenses—bank cards are the obvious choice. Finally, bank card networks possess a psychological and economic lever that stablecoins currently lack: consumer rewards. In the US and other mature markets, the status of "preferred payment method" is earned through cash back, airline miles, and loyalty points. These rewards are funded by transaction fees ($0.30 + 2-3%) that make payment gateways expensive. Bank cards essentially "incentivize" users to use them rather than other alternatives, especially in high-value transactions. Recent protocols are expanding this existing structure to more directly support AI agents. Stripe and OpenAI's Agent Commerce Protocol (ACP), Visa's Trusted Agent Protocol (TAP), and Mastercard's Agent Pay are all extensions to the existing bank card ecosystem designed to make automated transactions (by agents) trustworthy. ACP focuses on enabling agents to transact through existing merchant payment processes by integrating identity, authorization, and payment execution into a standardized protocol that merchants and payment providers can verify. Visa's TAP framework is designed to work in conjunction with its Visa Token Service (VTS), allowing merchants to verify whether a purchase was executed on behalf of the user by an AI agent authorized by the user. Mastercard's Agent Pay also leverages tokenization and programmable spending controls, allowing issuers to bind payment tokens to transaction limits, authorization windows, and predefined usage policies to ensure agents do not exceed their permissions or intentions. In practice, these approaches converge on a similar pattern: AI agents use tokenized bank card credentials for transactions, while the protocol layer verifies the agent's identity and permissions. Spending limits, authorization windows, and other policy controls are then enforced by the issuing bank, which can directly bind these rules to the payment credentials. The result is a system in which agents can operate autonomously while still being bound by the same rules, controlled by the card issuer, that are still used today to manage bank cards. Therefore, "just use a bank card" is the right advice for most agentic tasks. If your agent is doing what humans would do, then use the infrastructure that humans are using. But agents are not just faster humans. They are another type of economic actor, and this changes what transactions might occur. Humans wouldn't fill out a payment form for a $0.003 API call because the friction cost outweighs the value itself. Nor would humans approve 1,000 microtransactions per hour, or continuously rebalance positions every 30 seconds. These transactions don't exist in human commerce not because they're technically infeasible, but because no one would bother. Intelligent agents eliminate this friction. Cognitive overhead is reduced to zero. However, eliminating human friction is not the same as eliminating economic friction. Bank card channels have a fixed cost floor: approximately $0.30 to $0.50 per transaction, plus a 2-3% fee. This bottom line exists because each layer of a multi-party settlement architecture takes a cut: the issuing bank, the acquiring bank, the card network, and the processor. This cannot be eliminated through engineering design. Furthermore, bank card fees vary depending on card type, merchant category, and transaction volume. For example, with 1000 API calls per hour, a smart agent using a bank card would pay approximately $300 to $500 in processing fees. A stablecoin smart agent using the x402 protocol would pay less than $1 to $10 (on Solana or Base). In this new type of transaction, the buyer protection model is also reversed. The bank card network prioritizes the buyer: the seller bears the costs of fraud protection, refund risk, and compliance expenses. Stablecoins are seller-first: settlement is final, the seller gains certainty, and the buyer waives recourse. This reversal is usually a disadvantage for stablecoins. However, it becomes an advantage when certain conditions are met: Buyers don't need protection. Transaction amounts are extremely small, delivery is instant and verifiable, or payment is voluntary. The burden on sellers at the bank card level is high enough to completely impede transactions: minimum fees per transaction exceed the payment amount, refunds for consumed digital goods would result in irreparable losses, or sellers simply cannot access traditional payment infrastructure. (Annually, refunds cost merchants as much as $117 billion. The direct cost of a refund globally is approximately $25-33 billion, but the overall impact on merchants—including lost goods, shipping costs, processing fees, penalties, and the operational costs of handling disputes—pushes it into the $117 billion range. Refund mechanisms are particularly ineffective for digital goods and consumed services. If someone uses $50,000 worth of computing resources and then disputes that fee, those resources are exhausted. The seller not only bears the cost but now also the loss from the refund.) Intelligent agents greatly expand the areas where these two conditions overlap, for example: Small payments. A programmable agent pays a data provider $0.003 for each API call. The buyer doesn't need to provide refund protection for less than a penny; if the API returns junk data, the call can simply be stopped. Verifiable digital goods. An agent purchases datasets or computation results whose quality can be verified programmatically within milliseconds. When verification is instantaneous, you don't need refund protection. Physical goods require this protection because you need to wait days to discover if they are defective. Digital goods, however, largely do not. Metering services and elastic computing. A $5 computing task is manageable. The seller can bear the risk of refunds. But if usage surges from $5 to $50,000 in a single day, providing $50,000 worth of resources, and then there's the risk of losses due to fraud disputes, that's a real problem. Stablecoins allow you to finalize transactions in frequent, small increments. Usage can scale, but the risk of fraud borne by the seller doesn't have to scale accordingly. Long-tail cross-border services. A developer in Lagos provided a niche API that a startup in Berlin wanted to use. Sellers may not be able to use Stripe/Visa (available in many countries is limited), cannot afford the setup costs of a merchant account, or handle KYC compliance expenses for a side business. These are not marginal cases. They represent a growing business category that card payment gateways cannot structurally access. My point is simple: for agency-based businesses, cards win; for machine-native transactions that humans will never execute or that card payment gateways cannot handle, stablecoins win. Stablecoin Technology Stack: An Initial Expansion

Over the past two years, a complete technology stack has quietly emerged to support native payments by smart agents. It comprises four layers.

First is the settlement infrastructure. Bank card authorization is a complex process: from the merchant terminal to the acquiring bank, then to the card network, and finally to the issuing bank, the actual settlement takes T+1 to T+3 days to complete. For smart agents waiting for bank card authorization, each transaction takes several seconds, while funds are tied up in transit for several days.

In contrast, stablecoin transactions settle in seconds on the EVM chain and less than a second on Solana, providing instant finality rather than a pending state, which is highly advantageous for merchants. An agent can execute thousands of stablecoin transactions in the time it takes to process a single bank card payment settlement. As part of this, there's also the payment protocol: x402. x402 is a micropayment protocol native to HTTP that allows agents to pay for each API call with stablecoins. It requires no merchant account, bank card network, or acquiring bank. Coinbase's implementation on Solana costs $0.00025 per transaction. Since May 2025, over 50 million transactions have been settled across all supported chains. x402 is a great start, and I expect new infrastructure solutions to emerge in the near future. Secondly, there's the wallet and policy infrastructure. Bank cards excel at permission management, but stablecoins can do the same with the help of a wallet-layer policy engine. Coinbase's Agentic Wallets (launching in February 2026) implements gas-free transactions and programmable spending limits on Base. Furthermore, policy layers led by Turnkey, Privy, and Safe allow developers to directly encode dynamic allow lists, spending caps, and merchant-level routing rules into the wallet. These safeguards, similar to the bank card technology stack, ensure that agent permissions are constrained by the infrastructure, rather than relying on the reliability of the LLM/skills/orchestrator itself. Finally, the most innovative but yet fully utilized part is agent identity: ERC-8004. The Trustless Agents standard (launching in January 2026) is working to build an on-chain reputation infrastructure for smart agents, and in its first few months, 83,000 smart agents have registered on 18 networks. Its goal is to achieve native credit scoring for smart agents, as under-collateralized credit does not yet exist on-chain, but I believe ERC-8004 will bring about change.

Built for missions, not for factions

The market has already foreshadowed this shift. In February 2026, ACP architect Stripe added support for x402 to Base, effectively connecting smart agents that natively use bank cards with stablecoin settlements. ...p>

data.title: How will the wave of redemptions from private credit funds affect the financial and crypto markets?
data.description:
Written by: jsai@Jinse Finance

In March 2026, in a corner unseen by the US-Iran war, the US private lending market suddenly faced a peak in redemption pressure.

BlackRock's flagship fund, the HPS Corporate Lending Fund (HLEND), with $26 billion in assets under management, announced that it would strictly limit quarterly redemptions to 5%, despite investor redemption requests reaching 9.3% (approximately $1.2 billion in requests, with only about $620 million approved).

This is not an isolated case; funds under leading institutions such as Blackstone, Blue Owl, Cliffwater, and Morgan Stanley have also faced similar predicaments.

I. Which funds have already implemented redemption restrictions?

Currently, it has not reached the point of a "general" industry-wide mandatory shutdown of redemptions, but the pressure is highly concentrated on "semi-open" or interval funds (BDC) targeting retail and high-net-worth clients.

These funds typically offer a redemption window of up to 5% of net asset value each quarter. If applications exceed this limit, the manager can legally initiate a restriction mechanism. Funds known to have begun restricting redemptions include: BlackRock HPS Corporate Lending Fund (HLEND, $26 billion): Applications in Q1 2026 were 9.3%, strictly limited to 5%, triggering the "structural cap" for the first time. This is also the first large institution to explicitly restrict perpetual private lending products since the recent market turmoil. Blackstone BCRED (approximately $82 billion): Applications reached a record 7.9%, exceeding the usual 5% cap. The company used approximately $400 million of its own funds and those of its executives to fully meet redemptions, without officially "closing the gate," but demonstrating significant buffering pressure. Blue Owl Capital's OBDC II and other BDCs ($1.6-1.9 billion): Starting in February, regular quarterly redemptions ceased, replaced by periodic distributions (loan repayments or asset sales), effectively a restriction. Previously, it had repurchased 15.4% of its holdings, and the stress test revealed its first cracks. Cliffwater Corporate Lending Fund ($33 billion): Facing redemption requests exceeding 7%, the window just closed on Tuesday (March 10th), and the company has not yet decided whether to limit it to 5% or increase it to 7%. Morgan Stanley North Haven Private Income Fund ($8 billion): With requests around 11%, North Haven returned approximately $169 million after setting a 5% redemption cap, less than half of the investor redemption requests. Other funds such as Ares and Fidelity have also seen increased redemption rates, but have not yet officially imposed restrictions. Overall, in the $1.8-2 trillion private lending market, retail-oriented funds are the hardest hit, while institutional funds are relatively stable, but the risk of confidence spillover has already emerged.

II. Why Restrict Redemptions: The Root Cause Lies in Structural Mismatch and Market Concerns

The core selling point of private lending is "high returns + low volatility + quarterly liquidity," but the underlying assets are long-term, non-public, and difficult-to-liquidate corporate loans (especially SaaS, technology LBOs, etc.).

Although many funds have explicitly stipulated a redemption window of up to 5% of net asset value, restricting redemptions is compliant and legal, when a wave of redemptions hits, funds face the shattering of their "liquidity illusion."

Factors triggering the redemption wave include: AI Impact: Many funds heavily invest in software company mortgage loans; generative AI could destroy their business models, leading to valuation downgrades. JPMorgan Chase has already downgraded the valuation of some software mortgage loans and tightened lending. Duration Mismatch: Investors can exit their funds quarterly, but loan terms are often several years. If excess redemptions are fully met, managers can only sell assets at a loss or increase leverage, harming the interests of remaining investors. BlackRock explicitly stated that "without the 5% cap, there will be a structural mismatch between investor capital and private credit loans." Loan Quality Concerns: The default rate has risen to 9.2% in 2025 (Fitch data). High-profile defaults (such as the UK's MFS fraud bankruptcy, with risk exposure exceeding £2 billion) exacerbate panic. Retail investor runs: In the past few years, retail investors' entry into the market has driven up its size. Now, with the Fed's interest rate cuts, the impact of AI, and macroeconomic uncertainty, retail investors are the first to withdraw, creating a "first come, first served" dilemma. When faced with a wave of redemptions, fund managers also face a dilemma. Fully satisfying redemptions would damage their reputation and long-term discipline; strict restrictions could trigger more panic redemptions. Blackstone chose to "pay out of its own pocket" to buffer the losses, BlackRock adhered to the terms, and Cliffwater is still weighing its options. III. Impact on financial markets: Systemic risks are emerging, similar to the eve of 2007? Private lending has become the core of "shadow banking," approaching $2 trillion in size. If a liquidity crisis spreads, it will have the following impacts: Asset management giants' stock prices plummet: BlackRock's stock price fell more than 7% after its announcement, and alternative asset management firms such as Blackstone, KKR, and Ares are collectively under pressure, marking their worst performance in a decade this year. Asset sell-offs and deleveraging: If more funds are forced to sell loans at bargain prices, it will depress valuations in the public bond market, and banks will tighten leverage financing for private lending (JPMorgan Chase has already taken action). Confidence crisis spillover: The collapse of retail investor confidence may affect the entire alternative asset class, leading to increased discounted trading in the secondary market. Analysts have likened this to the "canary moment" of the 2007 subprime crisis—an early sign of liquidity depletion and valuation opacity. In the short term, managers can avoid an immediate collapse by using their own funds or selling assets as a buffer; however, if the default rate continues to rise or the economy slows down, systemic risks will be amplified. IV. Impact on the Crypto Market: Indirect Transmission Predominates, RWA Becomes a Key Channel The direct impact of the private lending crisis on the crypto market is limited, but there are two spillover paths: Macroeconomic Risk Aversion and Deleveraging: Private lending deleveraging may trigger a wider sell-off of risky assets, with high-beta assets such as Bitcoin bearing the brunt. Historical experience shows that traditional financial liquidity tightening is often accompanied by a crypto "de-risking" market. RWA and On-Chain Transmission in DeFi: The current on-chain private lending scale is close to $5 billion, mainly entering DeFi in the form of tokenized lending. If the underlying traditional loans are impaired or defaulted, fluctuations in RWA net worth will trigger DeFi liquidations and liquidity tightening. Analysts warn that this could be transmitted to the crypto ecosystem through two channels: "macro deleveraging + tokenized credit products." However, overall, the crypto market is more affected by "emotional contagion" than by direct asset linkages. Short-term volatility may increase, but long-term performance depends on Federal Reserve policy and the actual implementation of AI economy. This also serves as a warning that the crypto market must pay close attention to the underlying assets of RWA during the process of putting real-world assets (RWA) on-chain. Once the underlying assets encounter problems, the so-called RWA tokenization and DeFi will be castles in the air. Conclusion: Beware of "liquidity illusion," private lending enters a stress test period. This wave of redemptions is not the end of private lending, but rather its first real liquidity test after rapid expansion. While the adherence to caps by giants like BlackRock has protected funds in the short term, it tests investor trust in the long run. Financial markets need to pay attention to subsequent quarterly reports (more funds will disclose their results in March and April), while the crypto market should closely monitor RWA default signals and macro liquidity. For investors, it is advisable to carefully assess their own liquidity needs and avoid chasing the "high-yield illusion." Regulators may strengthen retail access scrutiny to prevent excessive risk transfer to retail investors. Whether this crisis is a "canary in the coal mine" or a "temporary pain" will be answered by data in 3-6 months. Markets always seek balance amidst volatility—the next chapter in private lending may well be a more transparent and sustainable one.

data.title: Those born after 1995 are already "old": AI recruitment is "burying" the middle management.
data.description:
Author: Ada, Deep Tide, TechFlow

“A major internet company offered 60 AI-background PhD graduates with annual salaries exceeding 3 million RMB this year.” Xiao Mafeng, founder of TTC, a headhunting firm that has served over 1500 AI companies, stated this number in a very calm tone, as if reporting the day's temperature.

In the same month, data from Maimai showed that the number of AI job postings surged 29 times, and Zhaopin.com reported a 200% increase in job seekers. 29 times more job postings, 200% influx—the numbers look like a bull market candlestick chart.

But these figures conceal a secret: a massive influx of capital and attention is flowing into a funnel with an extremely narrow opening. A few dozen individuals at the top have inflated salary expectations across the entire market, while hundreds of thousands at the bottom bear the brunt of all the anxiety. Meanwhile, in the middle of the funnel, those who have been in the workforce for five or ten years are being quietly drained. The booming talent market is a facade; the illusion of liquidity is real. A skilled general is hard to find, while countless soldiers fight tooth and nail. A Liepin report shows that 47% of AI positions require a master's or doctoral degree, and nearly half of the companies only recognize graduates from 985/211 universities. Headhunter Eva put it more bluntly: "Big companies are hiring; a 211 university degree is barely acceptable, at least a 985 university degree is required, and they basically don't even look at resumes without vertical project experience." What does the top tier look like? On the day the news of Lin Junyang's departure from Alibaba's Qianwen was released, "people from all sorts of big companies came to us, asking if we could help them contact Lin Junyang," Xiao Mafeng recalled. There are probably only a few dozen talents of this caliber nationwide. To find them, headhunters have long since stopped looking at resumes. They spend their time on GitHub checking code commit records, following paper authors on Google Scholar, and infiltrating podcast listener groups and AI startup communities. Eva even joined an AI startup competition group at Tsinghua University, which was full of young people in their late twenties. "We're talking now so that when they need jobs in two or three years, we can reserve a spot." Another headhunter, Steve, who started recruiting in the AI ​​field in 2022, made a thought-provoking remark: "I seriously doubt that there will be no resumes in the future." He gave an example. In January of this year, a company wanted to hire someone knowledgeable in OpenClaw. The field was too new, and no one would put it on a resume. His approach was to break down the requirement. Essentially, it was a multi-agent framework problem. Had anyone developed a similar framework? Was this framework open source? Who were the contributors in the open-source community? Resumes are depreciating in value, and traditional recruitment channels are becoming ineffective. Someone seized an opportunity from this crack. DINQ co-founder Sam's entrepreneurial journey began with a similar observation: the top authors of OpenAI papers were often not from prestigious universities, some even dropped out, were young, lacked academic titles, and were not easily understood by those outside the tech world. LinkedIn's logic of looking at education and resumes doesn't work for AI talent. Therefore, Sam created DINQ, a "LinkedIn for AI scientists and developers," focusing on achievements rather than resumes: top conference paper citations, GitHub code contributions, and whether collaborators are top-tier technical experts. When HR enters "Sora 2" on DINQ, the platform expands to include authors of papers related to that technology, rather than limiting itself to Sora 2-related experience, thus uncovering hidden talents. Xiao Mafeng's alternative is "build in public": simply release your product directly; that's the best proof of your capabilities. Although 621 universities have already established undergraduate programs in artificial intelligence, McKinsey predicts a 4 million AI talent gap in China by 2030. However, the term "gap" is misleading.
