What's lacking are experimental scientists who have trained on 100,000 GPUs, and multi-skilled talents who understand the limits of large-scale models and can find commercial applications. People who say "I'm particularly interested in AI" after listening to two podcast episodes are never in short supply in the market. Ye Xiangyu, founder of Nowcoder, aptly summarizes it: at the top, "a general is hard to find," while at the bottom, "ten thousand soldiers fight tooth and nail." The statement on Maimai that "one suitable candidate can be matched for every two AI positions" refers to the top. What about the bottom? No one keeps track of it because resumes at the bottom of the pyramid don't even get a chance to enter the system. Leveraged Pricing: The Closer to the Model, the More Valuable. So where does the money actually go? Eva provided some figures. At a major company, a P7 level non-technical position has a ceiling of around 1 million RMB. A comparable AI technical position earns 1.5 million to 2 million RMB. The difference in salary increases between job hopping is even greater; a 50% increase is common for technical positions, and some even double; non-technical positions increase by 10% to 20%, with a maximum of no more than 30%. Steve used one word to explain this pricing logic: leverage. Imagine the model as the sun. The closer someone is to the core, the greater the leverage they can exert, and the higher their value. An improvement in model capabilities made by a core researcher could impact a large company's market capitalization by billions. The cost of the 100,000 cards they operate far exceeds their own salary. From this perspective, even giving them 100 million wouldn't be expensive. What about those farther from the sun? Product managers, operations, and sales—the leverage effect isn't as direct, and their salaries are naturally limited. Steve estimates that at the application layer, the salary gap between technical and non-technical positions is more than two to three times. Xiao Mafeng adds a crucial variable. He believes this "hierarchy of contempt" is essentially supply and demand, and it has two layers. On a macro level, only a handful of people have done tens of thousands of calories of training, and their salaries are astronomical. But on a micro level, it depends on the founding team's DNA. If the founder is a professor at Tsinghua University, there are plenty of technical talents in the lab, but those who can commercialize the technology are more valuable. The scarcity of a few dozen people defines the salary narrative of the entire industry. Those who follow this narrative as a benchmark only find a gap between their expectations and reality. A purge of "Old Deng" "The AI ​​era rejects Old Deng," Xiao Mafeng offered a sharp commentary. Those who were in the last wave of AI, experiencing the era of Megvii and SenseTime, are now probably in their forties, and their experience has become a burden. Steve's statement was more subtle but consistent: "We don't believe in finding new continents with old maps. People who have worked in an industry for too long have great potential and inertia. The brain's direct reaction is the result of intensive training, but times have changed, and the correct reaction may be completely the opposite." Age anxiety has permeated every level. Some investment institutions are hunting for Gen Z entrepreneurs, and statements like "Those born after 1995 are already old" are starting to appear. This sounds absurd, but the signal from the recruitment market is very real: when resources are limited, the scales tip unhesitatingly towards young people. "Now the competition is about the speed of execution and implementation; everyone is training special forces, not large formations," Steve said. Special forces don't need so many commanders. But here's a contradiction no one wants to answer directly. What truly brings AI products to market and transforms technology into commercial value is precisely industry experience, tacit knowledge, and the lessons learned from past mistakes. Steve himself admits that this tacit knowledge exists in more mature individuals. They may not know which path they'll take, but they know which paths are definitely not viable. The industry needs the drive of young people, but it also needs the judgment of veterans. Everyone can say these two things, but the flow of money only addresses the first half. The middle layer has been swallowed up. Three headhunters unanimously mentioned a change: Management is being compressed. "It's already quite difficult for pure managers. A lot of things are being disrupted; the system you built might be overturned tomorrow," said Steve. Organizations are becoming extremely flat, no longer needing the pyramid structure of layered reporting, but rather small teams where everyone can fight. Relying on an agent to do something is less effective than relying on a person to do it. The emphasis used to be on strong management skills and managing complex teams, but this is being challenged. The boundaries between product managers, operations, and front-end/back-end engineers are blurring. One person can run an entire product's MVP using AI. Chen Lei (pseudonym) worked as a product director at a mid-sized AI company for three years, managing an eight-person team. At the beginning of this year, the company restructured, and her team was disbanded; four people were reassigned to Agent products, and two were laid off. Her own title changed from "director" to "senior product manager," reporting to a technical lead five years her junior. “I wasn’t laid off, but I know this is even worse than being laid off,” she said. “What you’ve built up over three years at this company is gone with just one organizational restructuring. And you can’t complain, because people will say, ‘Aren’t you still here?’” This is the cruelest part of this illusion of job mobility. At the top of the funnel, dozens of geniuses are being snapped up at exorbitant prices. At the bottom, hundreds of thousands of newcomers can’t even get in the door. And in the middle of the funnel, those who have been in the workplace for five, ten, or even fifteen years are being sidelined from within. Several rungs of the career ladder have been removed. Before, you could take an elevator, going up floor by floor; now it’s like skydiving—either landing directly at the top or freefalling. Who is creating this illusion? Who benefits from this illusion of liquidity? Recruitment platforms use headlines like "AI job postings surge 29 times" and "talent gap of 4 million" to garner traffic, pushing more anxious job seekers into the funnel with every repost. Companies use AI as a fig leaf. Forrester Research found that 55% of employers admit to regretting layoffs due to AI, as the AI ​​skills being replaced were never prepared. A survey by Resume.org is even more direct: 59% of companies admit to packaging layoffs as "AI-driven," arguing it sounds better to stakeholders. Saying it's because of AI sounds like a strategic upgrade; saying it's because of poor performance sounds like management failure. AI has become the best fig leaf. Klarna laid off 700 people, saying AI replaced customer service, but service quality plummeted, customers revolted, and they were quietly brought back. This is not an isolated case. Forrester predicts that half of the AI ​​layoffs will eventually be quietly rehired, but at lower salaries, or outsourced overseas. Steve aptly summarized the current mindset of bosses: "The first question they ask now is, 'Should we hire?' And then, what kind of people should we hire?" According to Forrester Research, only 16% of employees globally have high AI readiness. Companies don't invest in training; employees rely on self-learning. Generation Z has the highest AI readiness at 22%, yet they are the first to be eliminated from entry-level positions, because these are precisely the positions most easily swallowed up by AI. A Mercer survey shows that employee anxiety about AI-induced unemployment will surge from 28% in 2024 to 40% in 2026. AI is both a reason to poach talent and an excuse to lay off employees. Whoever holds the power to define it is the dealer in this game. The funnel won't widen. Let's go back to the initial set of numbers. A 29-fold increase in jobs, a 200% surge in job seekers, an annual salary of 3 million, and a talent gap of 4 million. Each number is real, but put together, they tell a completely different story: jobs are increasing, but the pool is extremely narrow; job seekers are flooding in, but the vast majority can't even pass the initial screening; salaries are soaring, but only a few dozen people at the very top of the pyramid receive them; the talent gap is widening, and the supply and demand are completely mismatched. But this funnel won't widen. AI technology iterates every six months; what's hottest today might be obsolete in six months. You think you're close to the sun, but a new model release can leave you relegated to the periphery. Steve said something that could serve as both an epitaph and an entry ticket for the industry: "Measuring seniority by length of time is probably no longer enough. What matters is the density and depth of your interaction with AI. Some people entered the industry four years ago but only used it casually. Some people only joined last year but are fully committed. Tell me, who has more seniority?" The three headhunters themselves are also being reshaped by this industry. Eva is learning algorithm principles, Steve is researching Agent frameworks, and Xiao Mafeng just came from a conference for Gen Z entrepreneurs, marveling that "their understanding has reached the next level." Even those selling shovels have to keep up with the pace of gold mining. Chen Lei recently started a small project on GitHub, writing an automatic legal document generation tool using the Agent framework. No one asked her to do it, and no one paid her. She said she'd figured something out: rather than waiting to be filtered through the funnel, it's better to carve out your own hole. This might be the only slightly optimistic part of the whole text, but even that's only marginally optimistic. The vast majority of people are neither among the 60 PhDs who received 3 million yuan, nor are they like Chen Lei, who have the ability and willingness to carve out their own holes. They are the silent majority in the middle of the funnel; those who aren't top-tier enough can be snapped up at exorbitant prices, and those who aren't decisive enough can start over. This funnel won't widen.

data.title: Jay Clayton's Crypto Wars
data.description:
Author: Lucas Matney Source: bankless Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Elizabeth Warren's style is refreshingly straightforward. The senator openly admits her aversion to the crypto industry, and her actions confirm this. She makes no secret of being an enemy of the industry.

But Jay Clayton is completely different.

This Trump-appointed Attorney for the Southern District of New York continues to launch a series of cases, clearly demonstrating his hostility towards the crypto industry and its developers. Yet, with the president's full support ("Jay Clayton is brilliant!!!"), almost no one in the industry is willing to publicly criticize him by name.

Jay's Legacy of Crypto Enforcement

Since Trump's first term as Chairman of the Securities and Exchange Commission, Jay Clayton has never truly stood on the side of the crypto industry:

  • Long before Gary Gensler systematized it, he pioneered a "enforcement-over-regulation" model for crypto regulation.

  • He relentlessly cracked down on ICO fundraising, forcing the industry into an era of airdrops and points, from which we are only recently slowly emerging.

  • He seized every opportunity to block the approval of a Bitcoin ETF.

  • Before taking over Gensler, Clayton's last action was to sue Ripple, accusing it of issuing securities without registration (while the court ultimately ruled that XRP itself was not a security). In short, Jay Clayton paved the way for Gensler. However, the difference is that Gensler's era of influence has ended, and Trump reappointed Clayton to a senior position last year. Despite Trump's public calls for regulators to take a completely opposite approach during his second term, Clayton continues to pursue enforcement actions against crypto developers. This reality was highlighted again yesterday: Jay Clayton requested a retrial of two charges against Roman Storm, the developer of Tornado Cash. Storm is the developer of the crypto mixing protocol. Although the 2023 indictment against the Tornado Cash developers was initiated by officials during the Biden administration, Jay Clayton, after taking office, spared no effort in sending the project's developers to prison. This all seems somewhat absurd, because in 2025, the Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) lifted sanctions on the Tornado Cash smart contract, which was the prerequisite for the Southern District of New York prosecutor to indict the developers. Logically, Clayton should have dropped the charges by then, but he didn't. Trump has never publicly commented on the case, and crypto and AI giant David Sachs has remained silent. In August, Storm was convicted of operating an unlicensed money transfer business (Clayton also forced the Samorai Wallet developers to plead guilty to the same charge last year), but the jury failed to reach a unanimous verdict on money laundering and dismissed the sanctions circumvention charges. Now, Jay Clayton wants to do it all over again—attempting to set a disastrous legal precedent for crypto developers. We must speak out more loudly. It's commendable that almost all major crypto industry lobbying groups are currently investing heavily in protecting crypto developers and fighting to end the abuse of federal funds transfer permit regulations. But we need to hear more from crypto executives, investors, and builders. Despite Trump's large following and numerous donors in the crypto world supporting his campaign and his family's various crypto businesses, industry leaders have seemed cautious in using their political capital over the past year. Understandably, most have only publicly voiced support for issues affecting their own businesses, remaining almost completely silent on the Roman Storm case. Take a look at this (unofficial) list compiled by *The Defiant*: attendees of the Trump White House Crypto Summit in March 2025. Many of these 25 "industry heavyweights" have been very close to the Trump administration over the past year. However, after checking each one, I found that only one executive on the list publicly tweeted in support of Roman Storm. Even if I may have missed some public statements or podcast remarks, to the best of my ability, the only executive on the list who personally tweeted in support of Roman Storm's case is Matt Huang of Paradigm. His venture capital firm publicly voiced its support and directly donated to Storm's defense fund (Bankless also provided a donation). Trump proclaims himself the crypto president, which is beneficial to the industry as a whole. We didn't get everything we wanted, but at least in the initial legislative process, we received relatively fair treatment. However, even if Trump's appointees embraced crypto as a technology and asset class, it doesn't mean they understood the industry's core values. We have a responsibility to clearly communicate: we are a values-driven industry, and we expect a pro-crypto government that doesn't attack or erode those values. Privacy is not a crime. Developing open-source software is not a crime. Jay Clayton must hear this. More leaders in our industry must say it.

data.title: Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs postpone expectations of Fed rate cuts: later but more significant cuts.
data.description:
Author: Xiao Yanyan, Jinshi Data

Morgan Stanley stated that the Federal Reserve may resume rate cuts as early as June, but there is a risk that the oil price shock triggered by the Iran war will delay the next rate cut.

The bank's economists maintain their previous forecast that the Fed will implement two 25-basis-point rate cuts this year—in June and September, respectively, although rising energy prices may exacerbate inflation. However, they believe that the Fed may postpone the first rate cut to September or even December, both of which could push the next rate cut to 2027.

Morgan Stanley's chief U.S. economist, Michael Gapen, and his colleagues said in a report on Wednesday, "If the Fed listens to historical experience, ignores the price pressures from oil, and implements easing policies sooner than expected, then we believe we are in a good position." The U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran significantly boosted oil prices and impacted markets. This has led to doubts about when the Fed will begin easing monetary policy again; the futures market currently expects only one 25-basis-point rate cut this year, most likely at the October meeting. Despite U.S. President Trump's recent repeated statements that the war with Iran will end "soon," and the International Energy Agency's (IEA) agreement on Wednesday to release a record 400 million barrels of oil from emergency reserves, oil prices remain high, with Brent crude trading at around $90 per barrel. Morgan Stanley economists say that if oil prices fail to fall back to pre-conflict levels, the impact on overall inflation in 2026 will be greater, and the unemployment rate will remain slightly high until the end of 2028. Before Trump launched the war, oil prices were around $70 per barrel. Gapen and his colleagues stated, "The Fed will balance this contradiction in its dual mandate by easing policy to a greater extent than originally planned. Therefore, our second risk to the monetary policy outlook is that the Fed will cut rates later, but more." Economists stated, "In our view, current market pricing reflects a high degree of uncertainty related to the duration of the conflict, as well as a recognition that the Fed's response will only become clearer with the passage of data and time." Goldman Sachs also postponed its forecast for Fed rate cuts, now expecting 25 basis point cuts in September and December, citing rising inflation risks related to the Middle East conflict. Previously, the bank had expected rate cuts to begin in June, followed by another cut in September. Goldman Sachs stated in a report on Wednesday that "based on our higher revised inflation forecasts, starting rate cuts in June appears premature," adding that earlier rate cuts are still possible if the labor market weakens earlier and more severely than expected.
